volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Ghost (Section 1.1.1 - Section 1.3.170)

sections.label: Section 1.1.1
sections.original: Exi e culina sis foras , mastigia ,
sections.translation: Get out of the kitchen , will you ; out of it , you whip-scoundrel , who are giving me your cavilling talk amid the platters ; march out of the house , you ruin of your master . Upon my faith , if I only live , I ’ ll be soundly revenged upon you in the country . Get out , I say , you steam of the kitchen .

sections.label: Section 1.1.5
sections.original: exi , inquam , nidor , e culina . quid lates ?
sections.translation: Why are you skulking thus ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.6
sections.original: quid tibi , malum , hic ante aedis clamitatiost ?
sections.translation: Why the plague are you making this noise here before the house ? Do you fancy yourself to be in the country [*] ? Get out of the house ; be off into the country . Go and hang yourself . Get away from the door .

sections.label: Section 1.1.9_10
sections.original: em , hocíne volebas ?
sections.translation: ( Striking him .) There now , was it that you wanted ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.9_10b
sections.original: Perii . cur me verberas ?
sections.translation: I ’ m undone ! Why are you beating me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.11
sections.original: Quia vivis .
sections.translation: Because you want it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.11b
sections.original: Patiar . sine modo adveniat senex .
sections.translation: I must endure it . Only let the old gentleman return home ; only let him come safe home , whom you are devouring in his absence .

sections.label: Section 1.1.13
sections.original: Nec veri simile loquere nec verum , frutex ,
sections.translation: You don ’ t say what ’ s either likely or true , you blockhead , as to any one devouring a person in his absence .

sections.label: Section 1.1.15
sections.original: Tu urbanus vero scurra , deliciae popli ,
sections.translation: Indeed , you town wit , you minion of the mob , do you throw the country in my teeth ? Really , Tranio , I do believe that you feel sure that before long you ’ ll be handed over to the mill . Within a short period , i ’ faith , Tranio , you ’ ll full soon be adding to the iron-bound race [*] in the country .

sections.label: Section 1.1.20
sections.original: nunc , dum tibi lúbet licetque , pota , perde rem ,
sections.translation: While you choose to , and have the opportunity , drink on , squander his property , corrupt my master ’ s son , a most worthy young man , drink night and day , live like Greeks [*] , make purchase of mistresses , give them their freedom , feed parasites , feast yourselves sumptuously .

sections.label: Section 1.1.25
sections.original: haecine mandavit tibi , quom peregre hinc it , senex ?
sections.translation: Was it thus that the old gentleman enjoined you when he went hence abroad ? Is it after this fashion that he will find his property well husbanded ? Do you suppose that this is the duty of a good servant , to be ruining both the estate and the son of his master ? For I do consider him as ruined , when he devotes himself to these goings on .

sections.label: Section 1.1.30
sections.original: quo nemo adaeque iuventute ex omni Attica
sections.translation: A person , with whom not one of all the young men of Attica was before deemed equally frugal or more steady , the same is now carrying off the palm in the opposite direction . Through your management and your tutoring has that been done .

sections.label: Section 1.1.34
sections.original: Quid tibi , malum , me aut quid ego agam curatiost ?
sections.translation: What the plague business have you with me or with what I do ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: an ruri quaeso non sunt , quos cures , bovis ?
sections.translation: Prithee , haven ’ t you got your cattle in the country for you to look to ? I choose to drink , to intrigue , to keep my wenches ; this I do at the peril of my own back , and not of yours

sections.label: Section 1.1.38
sections.original: Quam confidenter loquitur .
sections.translation: Then with what assurance he does talk ! ( Turning away in disgust .) Faugh !

sections.label: Section 1.1.38b
sections.original: At te Iuppiter
sections.translation: But may Jupiter and all the Deities confound you ; you stink of garlick ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.original: germana inluvies , rusticus , hircus , hara suis ,
sections.translation: you filth unmistakeable , you clod , you he-goat , you pig-sty , you mixture of dog and she-goat .

sections.label: Section 1.1.41b
sections.original: Quid vis fieri ?
sections.translation: What would you have to be done ? It isn ’ t all that can smell of foreign perfumes , if you smell of them ; or that can take their places at table above their master ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.44_45
sections.original: neque tam facetis quam tu vivis victibus .
sections.translation: or live on such exquisite dainties as you live upon . Do you keep to yourself those turtle-doves , that fish , and poultry ; let me enjoy my lot upon garlick diet . You are fortunate ; I unlucky . It must be endured .

sections.label: Section 1.1.49_50
sections.original: meum bonum me , te tuom maneat malum .
sections.translation: Let my good fortune be awaiting me , your bad yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.1.51
sections.original: Quasi invidere mi hoc videre , Grumio ,
sections.translation: You seem , Grumio , as though you envied me , because I enjoy myself and you are wretched . It is quite my due . It ’ s proper for me to make love , and for you to feed the cattle ; for me to fare handsomely , you in a miserable way .

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: O carnuficium cribrum , quod credo fore ,
sections.translation: O riddle for the executioner [*] , as I guess it will turn out ; they ’ ll be so pinking you with goads , as you carry your gibbet [*] along the streets one day , as soon as ever the old gentleman returns here .

sections.label: Section 1.1.58
sections.original: Qui scis , an tibi istuc evenat prius quam mihi ?
sections.translation: How do you know whether that mayn ’ t happen to yourself sooner than to me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.59
sections.original: Quia numquam merui , tu meruisti et nunc meres .
sections.translation: Because I have never deserved it ; you have deserved it , and you now deserve it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.original: Orationis operam compendi face ,
sections.translation: Do cut short the trouble of your talking , unless you wish a heavy mischance to befall you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.62
sections.original: Ervom daturin estis , bubus quod feram ?
sections.translation: Are you going to give me the tares for me to take for the cattle ? If you are not , give me the money . Go on , still persist in the way in which you ’ ve commenced ! Drink , live like Greeks ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.original: este , ecfercite vos , saginam caedite .
sections.translation: eat , stuff yourselves , slaughter your fatlings !

sections.label: Section 1.1.66
sections.original: Tace atque abi rús . ego ire in Piraeum volo ,
sections.translation: Hold your tongue , and be off into the country ; I intend to go to the Piraeus to get me some fish for the evening . To-morrow I ’ ll make some one bring you the tares to the farm . What ’ s the matter ? Why now are you staring at me , gallows-bird ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: Pol tibi istuc credo nomen actutum fore .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ ve an idea that will be your own title before long .

sections.label: Section 1.1.71
sections.original: Dum interea sic sit , istuc actutum sino .
sections.translation: So long as it is as it is , in the meantime I ’ ll put up with that before long .

sections.label: Section 1.1.72
sections.original: Ita est . sed unum hoc scito : nimio celerius
sections.translation: That ’ s the way ; and understand this one thing , that that which is disagreable comes much more speedily than that which you wish for .

sections.label: Section 1.1.74
sections.original: Molestus ne sis nunciam , i rus , te amove .
sections.translation: Don ’ t you be annoying ; now then , away with you into the country , and betake yourself off .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: ne tu erres hercle praeterhac mihi non facies moram .—
sections.translation: Don ’ t you deceive yourself , henceforth you shan ’ t be causing me any impediment . ( Exit .)

sections.label: Section 1.1.76
sections.original: Satin abiit neque quod dixi flocci existumat ?
sections.translation: Is he really gone ? Not to care one straw for what I ’ ve said ! O immortal Gods , I do implore your aid , do cause this old gentleman of ours , who has now been three years absent from here , to return hither as soon as possible , before everything

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: periere , et aedis et ager ; qui nisi huc redit ,
sections.translation: is gone , both house and land . Unless he does return here , remnants to last for a few months only are left . Now I ’ ll be off to the country ; but look ! I see my master ’ s son , one who has been corrupted from having been a most excellent young man . ( Exit .)

sections.label: Section 1.2.84
sections.original: Recórdatus múltum et diú cogitávi
sections.translation: I ’ ve often thought and long reflected on it ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.85
sections.original: argúmentaque ín pectus ínstitui multa
sections.translation: and in my breast have held many a debate , and in my heart ( if any heart I have ) have revolved this matter , and long discussed it , to what thing I ’ m to consider man as like , and what form he has when he is born ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.90
sections.original: id répperi iam exémplum .
sections.translation: I ’ ve now discovered this likeness . I think a man is like unto a new house when he is born . I ’ ll give my proofs of this fact . ( To the AUDIENCE .) And does not this seem to you like the truth ? But so I ’ ll manage that you shall think it is so .

sections.label: Section 1.2.95
sections.original: profécto ita esse út praedicó vera víncam .
sections.translation: Beyond a doubt I ’ ll convince you that it is true what I say . And this yourselves , I ’ m sure , when you have heard my words , will say is no otherwise than just as I now affirm that it is . Listen while I repeat my proofs of this fact ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.100
sections.original: simúl gnarurís vos volo ésse hanc rem mécum .
sections.translation: I want you to be equally knowing with myself upon this matter . As soon as ever a house is built up , nicely polished off [*] , carefully erected , and according to rule , people praise the architect and approve of the house , they take from it each one a model for himself . Each one has something similar , quite at his own expense ; they do not spare their pains .

sections.label: Section 1.2.105
sections.original: átque ubi illo ímmigrat néquam homo , indíligens
sections.translation: But when a worthless , lazy , dirty , negligent fellow betakes himself thither with an idle family , then is it imputed as a fault to the house , while a good house is being kept in bad repair . And this is often the case ; a storm comes on and breaks the tiles and gutters ; then

sections.label: Section 1.2.110
sections.original: dóminus indíligens réddere aliás nevolt ;
sections.translation: a careless owner takes no heed to put up others . A shower comes on and streams down the walls ; the rafters admit the rain ; the weather rots the labours of the builder ; then the utility of the house becomes diminished ; and yet this is not the fault of the builder . But a great part

sections.label: Section 1.2.115
sections.original: mórem hunc induxérunt : si quid númmo sarcirí potest ,
sections.translation: of mankind have contracted this habit of delay ; if anything can be repaired by means of money , they are always still putting it off , and don ’ t ⋯ do it until the walls come tumbling down [*] ; then the whole house has to be built anew . These instances from buildings I ’ ve mentioned ; and now I wish to inform you how you are to suppose that men are like houses .

sections.label: Section 1.2.120
sections.original: primúmdum paréntes fabrí liberúm sunt :
sections.translation: In the first place then , the parents are the builders-up of the children , and lay the foundation for the children ; they raise them up , they carefully train them to strength , and that they may be good both for service and for view before the public . They spare not either their own pains or their cost ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.125
sections.original: nec súmptus ibí sumptuí esse dúcunt ;
sections.translation: nor do they deem expense in that to be an expense . They refine them , teach them literature , the ordinances , the laws ; at their own cost and labour they struggle , that others may wish for their own children to be like to them . When they repair to the army , they then find them some relation [*] of theirs as a protector .

sections.label: Section 1.2.131
sections.original: eátenus . abeunt á fabris . únum ubi emeritum est stipendium ,
sections.translation: At that moment they pass out of the builder ’ s hands . One year ’ s pay has now been earned ; at that period , then , a sample is on view how the building will turn out . But I was always discreet and virtuous , just as long as I was under the management of the builder .

sections.label: Section 1.2.135
sections.original: póstea quom ímmigravi íngenium in meum ,
sections.translation: After I had left him to follow the bent of my own inclinations , at once I entirely spoiled the labours of the builders . Idleness came on ; that was my storm ; on its arrival , upon me it brought down hail and showers , which overthrew my modesty and the bounds of virtue , and untiled them for me in an instant .

sections.label: Section 1.2.141
sections.original: póstilla optígere me néglegens fui .
sections.translation: After that I was neglectful to cover in again ; at once passion like a torrent entered my heart ; it flowed down even unto my breast , and soaked through my heart . Now both property , credit , fair fame , virtue , and honor

sections.label: Section 1.2.145
sections.original: déseruerunt : égo sum in usu fáctus nimio néquior .
sections.translation: have forsaken me ; by usage have I become much worse , and , i ’ faith ( so rotten are these rafters of mine with moisture ), I do not seem to myself to be able possibly to patch up my house to prevent it from falling down totally once for all , from perishing from the foundation , and from no one being able to assist me . My heart pains me , when I reflect how I now am and how I once was ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.150
sections.original: quó neque indústrior dé iuventúte erat
sections.translation: than whom in youthful age not one there was more active in the arts of exercise [*] , with the quoit , the javelin , the ball , racing , arms , and horses . I then lived a joyous life [*] ; in frugality and hardihood I was an example to others ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.155
sections.original: óptumi quique éxpetebant á me doctrinám sibi .
sections.translation: all , even the most deserving , took a lesson from me for themselves . Now that I ’ m become worthless , to that , indeed , have I hastened through the bent of my inclinations . ( He stands apart .)

sections.label: Section 1.3.157
sections.original: Iam prídem ecastor frígida non lávi magis lubénter
sections.translation: On my word , for this long time I ’ ve not bathed in cold water with more delight than just now ; nor do I think that I ever was , my dear Scapha , more thoroughly cleansed than now .

sections.label: Section 1.3.159
sections.original: Eventus rebus omnibus , velut horno messis magna
sections.translation: May the upshot of everything be unto you like a plenteous year ’ s harvest .

sections.label: Section 1.3.160b
sections.original: Quíd ea messis attinet ad meam lavationem ?
sections.translation: What has this harvest got to do with my bathing ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.161
sections.original: Nihilo plus quam lavatio tua ad messim .
sections.translation: Not a bit more than your bathing has to do with the harvest .

sections.label: Section 1.3.161b
sections.original: O Venus venusta ,
sections.translation: O beauteous Venus , this is that storm of mine which stripped off all the modesty with which I was roofed ; through which Desire and Cupid poured their shower into my breast ; and never since have I been able to roof it in .

sections.label: Section 1.3.165
sections.original: madent iam in corde parietes , periere haec oppido aedis .
sections.translation: Now are my walls soaking in my heart ; this building is utterly undone .

sections.label: Section 1.3.166
sections.original: Contempla , amabo , mea Scapha , satin haec me vestis deceat .
sections.translation: Do look , my Scapha , there ’ s a dear , whether this dress quite becomes me . I wish to please Philolaches my protector , the apple of my eye .

sections.label: Section 1.3.168
sections.original: Quin tu te exornas moribus lepidis , quom lepida tute es ?
sections.translation: Nay but , you set yourself off to advantage with pleasing manners , inasmuch as you yourself are pleasing . The lover isn ’ t in love with a woman ’ s dress , but with that which stuffs out [*] the dress .

sections.label: Section 1.3.170
sections.original: Ita me di ament , lepidast Scapha , sapit scelesta multum .
sections.translation: So may the Gods bless me , Scapha is waggish ; the hussy ’ s quite knowing . How cleverly she understands all matters , the maxims of lovers too !
